title: How Prime Minister Anutin Revived the Thai Right's Electoral Fortunes
content:
For the first time this century, Thailand’s conservatives have won a general election outright. On February 8th, voters awarded the Thai Pride Party of Anutin Charnvirakul, the new prime minister, a surprising but decisive victory. This unexpected result could lead to an unusual run of political stability in a country that has lacked it since 1997. However, it will likely delay the significant reforms to the monarchy and armed forces championed by the liberal opposition. Furthermore, it leaves core economic policy questions unanswered for the time being.

In recent years, Thai politics has been a three-cornered contest between conservatives, populists, and liberals. Mr. Anutin managed to break the deadlock by combining his personal charisma with a sharp instinct for what voters actually want. In rural areas, he utilized patronage politics by recruiting local political bosses and offering populist-style fiscal handouts. Meanwhile, he appealed to better-off urban voters by appointing capable technocrats to run key government ministries. This balanced approach allowed him to cobble together a strong majority in Parliament's lower house.

Thailand’s liberals, who recently regrouped into the new People’s Party, are left licking their wounds after the election. Although they won a large number of votes nationwide, their support was too heavily concentrated in cities and among the middle classes. Under Thailand’s electoral system, this geographical concentration punished them severely and kept them in the opposition. Moving forward, voters will have to wait and see how the new conservative coalition handles the drafting of a new constitution. It also remains to be seen whether Anutin’s economic policies will favor traditional prudence or populist spending.
